event: Stories of Saint Jerome — allegorical virtues
symbols: cross held by central figure — Faith, the virtue by which we believe in God
chalice — the Eucharistic cup, symbol of Faith or Hope in salvation
woman with child — Charity (Caritas), the greatest of the theological virtues (1 Corinthians 13:13)
ornate cartouche frame — the decorative setting placing these virtues within the sacred architecture of the church
description: An ornate gilded cartouche frame surrounds a fresco medallion showing three female figures seated on clouds. The central figure holds a large golden cross and a chalice. To the left, a woman holds two small children. To the right, a woman gazes upward with hands clasped in prayer. A cherub face crowns the cartouche above. The composition is set within an architectural surround of scrollwork and gilded moldings.
theological_description: This medallion from the Stories of St. Jerome cycle depicts the three theological virtues — Faith, Hope, and Charity — which Paul identifies as the enduring gifts: 'And now these three remain: faith, hope and love. But the greatest of these is love' (1 Corinthians 13:13). The cross and chalice identify Faith, the foundational virtue through which we receive salvation (Ephesians 2:8). The upward-gazing figure represents Hope, 'the anchor of the soul' (Hebrews 6:19). The woman with children embodies Charity, the self-giving love that mirrors God's own nature (1 John 4:8). Their placement in a Hieronymian context reflects Jerome's conviction that theological study must be animated by these virtues — knowledge without love is empty. These virtues are 'infused' by God, distinguishing them from natural moral virtues, and together they orient the Christian life toward its heavenly end.

scripture_refs.reason: The three theological virtues — faith, hope, and love — are the direct subject of the fresco
Salvation through faith, represented by the figure holding the cross
Hope as the anchor of the soul, represented by the upward-gazing figure
God is love — Charity as the supreme virtue reflecting the divine nature
